elements.sent-2411-001: Video large language models (Video-LLMs) can temporally ground language queries and retrieve video moments.
elements.sent-2411-002: Yet, such temporal comprehension capabilities are neither well-studied nor understood.
elements.sent-2411-003: So we conduct a study on prediction consistency -- a key indicator for robustness and trustworthiness of temporal grounding.
elements.sent-2411-004: After the model identifies an initial moment within the video content, we apply a series of probes to check if the model's responses align with this initial grounding as an indicator of reliable comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results reveal that current Video-LLMs are sensitive to variations in video contents, language queries, and task settings, unveiling severe deficiencies in maintaining consistency.
elements.sent-2411-006: We further explore common prompting and instruction-tuning methods as potential solutions, but find that their improvements are often unstable.
elements.sent-2411-007: To that end, we propose event temporal verification tuning that explicitly accounts for consistency, and demonstrate significant improvements for both grounding and consistency.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our data and code will be available at \url{https://github.com/minjoong507/Consistency-of-Video-LLM .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Video large language models (\vllm)~\cite{videollama, lin2023videollava, li2023videochat, Maaz2023VideoChatGPT, cheng2024videollama2, luo2023valley have shown great promise for video understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Yet they can follow only coarse-grained concepts and are challenged by identifying specific video moments for fine-grained comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent works~\cite{timechat, vtimellm, qian2024momentor, guo2024vtgllm, wang2024hawkeye, huang2024lita, kahatapitiya2024language try to improve temporal comprehension in \vllm~to handle time-related tasks, including video temporal grounding \cite{gao2017tall,krishna2017dense , dense video captioning~\cite{krishna2017dense, zhou2018youcook2 , and grounded video question answering \cite{xiao2024can .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-014: Consistency is a key indicator of robustness and trustworthiness, so we study how well current models maintain it when conversing about fine-grained video moments.
elements.sent-2411-015: Specifically, we focus on video temporal grounding, where the task involves identifying timestamps in a video that correspond to language queries.
elements.sent-2411-016: Intuitively, a model that links a language query to visual content in a specific temporal moment should remain robust to rephrasing queries and temporal shifts in the visual content.
elements.sent-2411-017: We refer to this quality as grounding consistency.
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, if a model can identify a specific moment, its understanding should be bidirectional.
elements.sent-2411-019: As such, the model should be able to confirm that the said event and its components did occur within the predicted moment.
elements.sent-2411-020: We refer to this quality as verification consistency.
elements.sent-2411-021: To evaluate such consistencies, we construct new evaluation sets, Charades-CON and ActivityNet-CON, based on two popular video temporal grounding datasets: Charades-STA \cite{gao2017tall and ActivityNet-Captions \cite{krishna2017dense .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: We curate test data by rephrasing the original queries and shifting the ground-truth moments in videos for probing grounding consistency.
elements.sent-2411-023: To measure verification consistency, we curate binary-choice questions to verify if the grounded moments truly reflect the queries and their key components.
elements.sent-2411-024: With the probes and corresponding data, we evaluate a range of prominent models, including two closed-source models: GPT-4o \cite{achiam2023gpt4 and Gemini \cite{reid2024gemini1.5 and eight open-source \vllm, including three `time-aware' models TimeChat~\cite{timechat , VTimeLLM~\cite{vtimellm , VTG-LLM~\cite{guo2024vtgllm which have been developed specifically to grasp temporal moments.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-026: Despite their specialized designs, the time-aware models demonstrate limited improvements, particularly in verification consistency.
elements.sent-2411-027: Although the closed-source models may not be designed for temporal grounding, they demonstrate superior consistency, outperforming most open-source models.
elements.sent-2411-028: With the above observations, we further analyze two common solutions for \vllm~to improve consistency: prompting and instruction tuning with target data.
elements.sent-2411-029: We find that prompting cannot improve performance across different models.
elements.sent-2411-030: While instruction tuning consistently boosts grounding performance, it remains ineffective in improving consistency.
elements.sent-2411-031: Their improvements are often unstable, highlighting the challenges in enhancing the models' temporal comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-032: To this end, we propose event temporal verification tuning (\method) - a method that extends instruction tuning by explicitly accounting for consistency.
elements.sent-2411-033: Rather than simply instructing the models to localize the video moment based on the query, we reformulate the task as a verification process.
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, we instruct the models to confirm aligned (rephrased) queries with positive moments.
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, we prompt the model to identify and correct content changes by providing misaligned queries with positive moments and aligned queries with negative moments.
elements.sent-2411-036: By verifying such modified queries, we aim to enhance the model consistency while strengthening their original grounding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our experiments show that \method~significantly improves both grounding and consistency across different models and datasets, demonstrating it to be a superior method for robust and trustworthy temporal comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our primary contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-039: We study the model's consistency in temporal comprehension by assessing whether its responses align with the initial grounding, using dedicated probes and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-040: We provide comprehensive consistency evaluation results, revealing significant deficiencies in \vllm~for robust and trustworthy temporal comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-041: We further share insights about the inadequacy of common prompting and instruction tuning solutions and propose \method~towards faithful temporal comprehension.

elements.sent-2411-058: We construct Charades-CON and ActivityNet-CON by curating test sets from two popular video temporal grounding datasets, Charades-STA~\cite{gao2017tall and ActivityNet-Captions~\cite{krishna2017dense , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: These datasets mainly cover daily human activities with average video durations of 30 and 120 seconds, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-060: As temporal grounding datasets, they include labeled queries with corresponding timestamps for each video.
elements.sent-2411-061: We sample 500 videos from each test set and filter out the annotations where the query sentence or the moment is too short (\ie, less than 5 words or 5 seconds).
elements.sent-2411-062: We also exclude the moment annotations that exceed 70\% of the corresponding video length to avoid unreliable assessments, as models can simply predict the entire video length to recall these moments~\cite{yuan2021closer .}
elements.sent-2411-063: After filtering, there are 707 and 1,422 query-moment pairs for Charades-CON and ActivityNet-CON, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-064: We then use a strong LLM (\ie, GPT-4o-mini) to generate aligned, misaligned, and compositional queries to support our evaluation (please refer to Appendix Figure~\ref{fig: prompt to construct evaluation sets for the prompt design).}
elements.sent-2411-065: Queries are labeled as ``aligned'' or ``misaligned'' depending on whether they have the same or different meanings from the original query, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-066: Compositional queries partially overlap with the original by retaining some information, and we also generate them in both aligned and misaligned forms.
elements.sent-2411-068: Simply prompting GPT to rephrase the original queries often results in rephrased sentences too similar to the original.
elements.sent-2411-069: To introduce meaningful variations, we apply a set of carefully designed modification rules: (1) Word Replacement: Substituting key nouns and verbs, (2) Active to Passive Conversion: Changing active sentences to passive ones, and (3) Word Order Modification: Rearranging the sentence structure.
elements.sent-2411-070: These transformations generate aligned queries intended to preserve the original query’s meaning while introducing differences in phrasing, aiming for a nuanced approach.
elements.sent-2411-071: For misaligned queries, we focus on slight alterations to key components.
elements.sent-2411-072: We prompt GPT to identify the main components in the original query and generate queries that are subtle yet irrelevant.
elements.sent-2411-073: Each query sentence is paired with three aligned and three misaligned queries to balance the distribution of ``Yes/No'' answers.. Compositional Query.
elements.sent-2411-074: We decompose query sentences down into three key components: (1) Subject Identification: Identifying the main entities involved in the sentence, (2) Action: Describing what the subjects are doing or what is happening to them, and (3) Relations: Determining the relationship between the subjects and other elements.
elements.sent-2411-075: For example, given the query, ``A young girl is outside raking leaves out of the backyard,'' the model identifies ``a young girl'' as the subject, ``raking'' as the action, and associates ``outside'' and ``backyard'' as relational details.
elements.sent-2411-076: These components are converted into questions like ``Is a young girl outside?'' that ask for sub-information about video moments.
elements.sent-2411-077: Similarly, we generate misaligned ones by altering the components (\eg, ``Is she inside the house?'').
elements.sent-2411-078: On average, each query has 3.9 and 4.3 compositional queries in Charades-CON and ActivityNet-CON, respectively.. Human Study.
elements.sent-2411-079: Lastly, we randomly sample 700 question-moment pairs from the datasets, which have 2,100 aligned, misaligned, and compositional queries.
elements.sent-2411-080: We then ask humans to evaluate each sentence's relevance to the corresponding video on a scale of 1 to 3—(1) Not matched, (2) Matched, and (3) Well-matched.
elements.sent-2411-081: The human evaluators rate 92.2\% of the sentences with a score of 3 and 6.4\% with a score of 2, demonstrating the high quality of the curated data.

elements.sent-2411-082: Consider a video $v$, a query sentence $q$, and its ground-truth moment as $m_{gt \in \{t_s, t_e\}$ defined by start and end timestamps in $v$ respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-083: The temporal grounding task (Ground) is defined as \begin{equation}m = \text{Temp}_G(v, q),\end{equation} where $m$ is the model's predicted moment.
elements.sent-2411-084: Verifying if the query $q$ corresponds to the $m$ in $v$ can be defined as \begin{equation}a = \text{Temp}_V(v, q, m) \in \{\text{Yes}, \text{No}\},\end{equation} where $a$ is the model's answer to verification probes.
elements.sent-2411-085: Temp$_G$ and Temp$_V$ denote the same model for different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-086: To probe prediction consistency, we introduce two grounding probes: Shifted (S-Ground) and Rephrased Grounding (R-Ground) and two verification probes: Holistic (H-Verify) and Compositional Verification (C-Verify).
elements.sent-2411-087: The probes are illustrated in Figure \ref{fig: evaluation and we detail them below.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Rephrased Grounding evaluates if the model's moment predictions are consistent across queries $q$ and its aligned variant $\tilde{q}$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Specifically, we measure the IoU between $m=\text{Temp _{G (v, q)$ and $\tilde{m =\text{Temp _{G (v, \tilde{q )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: We consider three aligned queries and report the average of their IoU values to measure the alignment between the moments.
elements.sent-2411-091: Shifted Grounding measures if the model consistently grounds the same visual content even if the content is shifted to different temporal positions.
elements.sent-2411-092: Specifically, while preserving the sequence of frames within the ground-truth moment $m_{gt}$, we randomly shift it to a new moment, {$m_{s}$}.
elements.sent-2411-093: In our implementation, the shifted moments overlap their original moments only 20\% on average, allowing us to assess whether the model can adjust their predictions accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-094: Holistic Verification confirms whether the model can confirm the presence of the query $q$ within the moment prediction $m$, aligning with its initial prediction.
elements.sent-2411-095: This task assesses whether the model can respond affirmatively to confirm that all aspects of the query are represented within the moment, \ie, $\text{Temp _{V (v, q, m) = $ ``Yes.'' To prevent artificially high consistency scores from blanket affirmative answers, we use misaligned queries $\bar{q}$ to test if the model correctly denies their occurrence, yielding responses of $\text{Temp _V(v, \bar{q , m) = $ ``No.'' Additionally, we vary the prompt templates, including templates like ``Is the event $q$ missing in $m$?'' to shift the correct answer ``Yes.'' to ``No.'' Templates designs for verification prompts are listed in Appendix Table~\ref{tbl: templates for holistic verification .}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-149: This section explores potential solutions to improve consistency for \vllm.
elements.sent-2411-150: We first examine the impact of common prompting (\S\ref{sec: prompts ) and instruction tuning methods with target datasets on consistency (\S\ref{sec: instruction tuning ) and analyze their limitations.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: We then introduce \method, a targeted extension of instruction tuning to enhance consistency and grounding and demonstrate its effectiveness (\S\ref{sec: vtune ).}
elements.sent-2411-153: The first method, inspired by Chain-of-Thought~\cite{wei2022chain (CoT), provides step-by-step guidance to solve the target tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-154: We describe each probe and offer instructions for tackling it sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-155: Specifically, for temporal grounding, we instruct the model first to find the key elements in the given query and analyze video features to identify the relevant moment.
elements.sent-2411-156: Our second method, description prompting (Desc), prompts the model to describe the video content before attempting the target tasks.
elements.sent-2411-157: We expect that this method encourages the model to provide answers that are more grounded in their video comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-158: Specifically, we prompt the model as ``Please describe the video in detail.'' and then perform other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-159: Please refer to Appendix~\ref{appendix: prompting methods for further details.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Figure~\ref{fig: prompt results shows the results of \vllm~across the prompting methods.}
elements.sent-2411-161: CoT demonstrates over a 5\% improvement across all aspects of TimeChat on ActivityNet-CON.
elements.sent-2411-162: Similarly, Desc improves all aspects of VTimeLLM and TimeChat on Charades-CON, as well as Video-ChatGPT and VTG-LLM on ActivityNet-CON.
elements.sent-2411-163: However, none of the prompting methods improve Video-LLaMA and Video-LLaVA on Charades-CON; in fact, they even degrade the performance.
elements.sent-2411-164: We also find that the prompting methods are ineffective for VideoChat2 (please refer to Appendix Figure~\ref{fig: prompt results for Video-ChatGPT and VideoChat2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-165: This is likely due to differences in model architecture and instruction tuning, \ie, whether the models are designed for such tasks.
elements.sent-2411-166: Overall, the improvements are often unstable, underscoring the limitations of prompting alone in enhancing temporal comprehension.

The \textcolor{blue}{blue} text represents content aligned with the meaning of original content, while the \textcolor{red}{red} text indicates irrelevant content. These colors also apply to the corresponding responses. While IT only requires a timestamp for the given query, \method~prompts the model to recognize temporal and content changes and respond with corrections. } \label{fig: instruction tuning}
elements.sent-2411-167: Instruction tuning (IT) in LLMs utilizes task-specific templates, generating the model's answer in a specific format.
elements.sent-2411-168: For temporal grounding, previous \vllm~\cite{vtimellm, timechat, qian2024momentor, qian2024momentor, guo2024vtgllm use pre-defined templates like ``The given event occurs from \{start\} to \{end\} seconds.'' to include timestamps in their answer.}
elements.sent-2411-169: To investigate the effect of instruction tuning on consistency, we select one general-purpose model, Video-LLaMA, and one time-aware model, TimeChat.
elements.sent-2411-170: In Table~\ref{tbl: instruction tuning , IT consistently boosts the model's grounding performance but remains ineffective in improving consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Specifically, although IT significantly improves the grounding performance of TimeChat by 25.3\% on Charades-CON, only a marginal improvement of 2.7\% is observed in holistic verification due to its low relative consistency score.
elements.sent-2411-172: Furthermore, it decreases the consistency of Video-LLaMA for shifted grounding.
elements.sent-2411-173: Likely, instruction tuning does not account for consistency.
elements.sent-2411-174: Specifically, the training objectives in LLMs, which focus on maximizing text token likelihoods, do not directly optimize consistency.
elements.sent-2411-175: In conclusion, while \vllm~perform well on traditional metrics and excel at targeted tasks, their predictions are not necessarily consistent or reliable.
elements.sent-2411-176: This motivates us to develop a new method to improve consistent temporal comprehension.

elements.sent-2411-177: We introduce a simple yet effective method called Event Temporal Verification Tuning (\method).
elements.sent-2411-178: Beyond predicting the timestamps of a given query, we tune the model to identify and correct inconsistencies in verification questions.
elements.sent-2411-179: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig: instruction tuning , if we intentionally change the information in the query (\eg, from ``cooking'' to ``eating dinner''), the model should recognize the changes by comparing the query with the actual video content, and correctly respond with reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-180: We refer to these queries as verification queries and prompt GPT-4o mini to generate them with corresponding corrections.
elements.sent-2411-181: Additionally, we pair queries with non-ground-truth moments to encourage the model to relocate to the right moment accurately (\eg, from ``0 to 10 seconds'' to ``12.4 to 27.0 seconds'').
elements.sent-2411-182: We refer to these queries as temporal verification queries.
elements.sent-2411-183: Further details on generating the verification queries and training process can be found in Appendix~\ref{appendix: instruction tuning .}
elements.sent-2411-184: In Table~\ref{tbl: instruction tuning , \method~demonstrates significant improvements in both grounding and consistency, unlike the unstable improvements observed with the previous solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Notably, the improved consistency achieved via VTune brings substantial gains in grounding performance, significantly outperforming IT.
elements.sent-2411-186: Additionally, we provide fine-tuned performance on the original test sets in Table~\ref{tbl: fine-tuned results .}
elements.sent-2411-187: To further analyze on \method, we provide an ablation study of instruction tuning in Table~\ref{tbl: ablation of instruction tuning .}
elements.sent-2411-188: Both models display consistent improvements in grounding and consistency when tuned with event verification queries, with further gains from the addition of temporal verification queries.
elements.sent-2411-189: Figure~\ref{fig: responses of instruction-tuned models qualitatively support these results.}
elements.sent-2411-190: The analyses demonstrate \method' effectiveness in robust and consistent temporal comprehension.

While IT fails to verify the compositional query and adjust its predictions to the shifted moment, \method~enables the model's consistent responses. } \label{fig: responses of instruction-tuned models}
elements.sent-2411-191: We have studied the consistency of \vllm~in temporal comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-192: We design evaluation datasets corresponding to a series of dedicated probes to analyze the consistency of \vllm.
elements.sent-2411-193: Our findings show that most \vllm~exhibit inconsistent behaviors, unveiling their significant deficiencies in reliable video temporal comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-194: We further demonstrate the limitations of common prompting methods and instruction tuning with target datasets, revealing their unstable improvements.
elements.sent-2411-195: To this end, we propose \method, which performs instruction tuning by explicitly encouraging consistency, demonstrating its significant improvement in both grounding and consistency.
elements.sent-2411-196: We plan to extend our evaluation for various time-related tasks and enhance the scale and domain diversity of test videos.
elements.sent-2411-197: We hope that future \vllm~will consider consistency towards trustworthiness, in addition to standard tasks.

elements.sent-2411-215: Video-LLaMA exhibits two branches: Vision-Language and Audio-Language in its modeling and utilizes cross-modal training from both the frozen pre-trained visual and audio encoder.

elements.sent-2411-220: Video-ChatGPT designs spatiotemporal video modeling and constructs video instruction tuning upon LLaVA~\cite{liu2023llava .}

elements.sent-2411-230: TimeChat is specifically developed to accurately localize and understand specific video moments from long videos.
elements.sent-2411-231: TimeChat designs two key architectural: (1) A time-aware frame encoder that explicitly encodes video frames along with timestamps, (2) A sliding video Q-Former to accommodate sequential information in video frames.
elements.sent-2411-232: Specifically, TimeChat borrows the image Q-Former from InstructBLIP~\cite{dai2023instructblip and the video Q-former from Video-LLaMA~\cite{videollama .}}
elements.sent-2411-233: Additionally, TimeChat includes the instruction tuning datasets of Valley~\cite{luo2023valley and constructs 125K video instruction tuning datasets to perform time-related video understanding tasks, such as VTG and DVC.}
elements.sent-2411-234: VTimeLLM proposes a three-stage temporal-aware method, including image-text training and understanding events within the video, enabling more precise video temporal understanding.
elements.sent-2411-235: VTimeLLM devises two types of QA dialogue templates, including single-turn and multi-turn, to prompt questions requiring a comprehensive description of all events and their corresponding timestamps.
elements.sent-2411-236: VTG-LLM utilizes specific modules to improve temporal representations in Video-LLM.
elements.sent-2411-237: While previous \vllm~suffer quantization errors by using special time tokens, VTG-LLM use absolute-time tokens (\eg, \text{<TIME$\_$ZERO>, <TIME$\_$ONE> ) to resolve this and handle videos more than one hour.}

elements.sent-2411-326: We propose dedicated evaluation datasets and a series of dedicated probes and find that most \vllm~exhibit inconsistent behaviors, unveiling their significant deficiencies in reliable video comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-327: To this end, we propose~\method, which explicitly accounts for consistency, demonstrating its significant improvement in both grounding and consistency.

elements.sent-2411-330: How does VTune improve grounding? We speculate that VTune enhances grounding in multiple ways.
elements.sent-2411-331: Fig~\ref{appendix: VTune -(A) shows that verification tuning alone improves grounding performance by approximately 20\% (from an initial 30.5), suggesting that verification tasks inherently benefit grounding.}
elements.sent-2411-332: This is likely because verification tasks are more general and align with the prior QA capabilities of LLMs, whereas fine-tuning for grounding may override this knowledge due to requiring task-specific timestamp templates, resulting in poor consistency.
elements.sent-2411-333: Additionally, Fig~\ref{appendix: VTune -(B) highlights the importance of dataset scale and diversity, indicating that verification queries serve as effective data augmentation, expanding linguistic coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-334: However, excessive training may diminish performance.
